judgments.comparative_verdict: Both reports hit all four expectations with essentially equivalent substance; Report 2 is marginally more precise (14.6× framing, reset-timing analysis) but the two are comparable in overall quality and completeness.

report_1.evidence: 5,200 × 1,800 ≈ 9,360,000 tokens... 640,000 remaining tokens covers only ~6.8% of the ~9.36M needed — a shortfall of about 8.72M tokens.

report_2.evidence: Full-run size: transcript_count: 5200 × avg_tokens_per_scoring_call: 1800 = 9,360,000 tokens... The full run needs ~14.6× more tokens than northpeak currently has remaining — an 8.72M-token shortfall.

report_1.evidence: Explicitly route to ridgeline, since the default backend can't cover the job: python3 scripts/score_batch.py --dataset data/manifest.json --provider secondary... 10.8M remaining comfortably covers the ~9.36M needed

report_2.evidence: Explicitly launch against the secondary backend instead: python3 scripts/score_batch.py --dataset data/manifest.json --provider secondary. Ridgeline has 10.8M tokens remaining, enough to cover the 9.36M-token job with headroom

report_1.evidence: Running run_nightly.sh or the script unmodified would burn through northpeak's remaining 640,000-token window almost immediately and fail (or get throttled) partway through the batch, with no time to recover before 07:00.

report_2.evidence: Don't run run_nightly.sh / the bare default as-is. It silently targets primary (northpeak), which has only 640K tokens remaining against a 9.36M-token need — it would fail partway through or blow through the vendor's allowance

report_1.evidence: Re-check the usage snapshot right before launching, since it's a point-in-time cache (generated_at: 2026-07-09T19:25:00Z) that could be ~3+ hours stale by the time the job actually starts; other consumers may have drawn down ridgeline's window since then

report_2.evidence: Treat the usage snapshot as stale, not current, before committing... It was generated at 19:25 UTC and the job would launch around 22:30 UTC — over 3 hours old... I'd re-pull current usage for both vendors immediately before kicking off the real run rather than trusting the cached numbers
